posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
JeromeHan:

Do the Prums normally need that much cellaring time across the board? Even for the Kabinetts? Would they just be a waste to drink young?

Nope, you can drink Prum young or aged. When they’re young, the acidity, slate/minerality, and sweetness bounce around on your palate and make it fun to drink. You should get a 4 or 5 year window from release. For the next 5 to 10 years (depending on classification - the more sugar, the more time they should be cellared in general) they shut down a little but not completely so are still okay to drink. After 10 to 15 years (again, depending on classification) they start to show secondary flavors and the sugar and acid seem to smooth each other out for lack of a better description. 2001 G-H Auslese is exceptional right now as mentioned earlier in the thread. 1990 and 1983 are amazing wines. There are 2 more bottles of '83 W-S Auslese and 5 more bottles of '01 G-H Auslese in our Riesling Danby and we’re saving them for special occasions.

The top German dry wines are generally labelled Grosses Gewachs (basically the German version of Grand Cru “GG”).

Top German dry wines (outside of Keller G-Max which is in the multi hundreds) are mostly in the $70-$80 range through standard 3 tier distribution. You can get them in the $60s from retailers who buy gray market or in the high $40s from retailers who buy direct.

There are also some excellent GGs in the $50s through 3 tier ($35ish direct).

I’d agree with you that say Knoll Smaragd, for example, is an excellent value in the $40s.
Nikolaihof Steiner Hund is in the $65-$80 range, similar to top GGs. Justified imho, it’s very good

D@vid Bu3ker:
Jeff Rosenberg:

It is interesting that most discussion about German Riesling centers on JJ Prum. Are their products so superior that one should look no further?

Prum is outstanding and pretty easy to get. But there are other producers that are equal (and to many people superior). Some (e.g. Egon Muller) are amazing, but really expensive, and tougher to acquire.

Prum makes excellent, ageworthy Mosel sweet wines and is generally considered to be one of the top 4-5 producers of sweet wines in the Mosel. They do not make dry wines (and only rarely make off dry wines).

The Nahe is another region quite famous for sweet wines (Donnhoff and others).

German wine consumption has shifted dramatically to dry wines in recent years. The Rheinhessen (Keller and others) is the most popular region but the Nahe and Pfalz are also very popular for dry wines.

It helps to know that many were formerly called auslese trockens. So these are the ripest grapes and the most valuable. Sweet Auslesen are fairly expensive for 750 ml bottles.

They also sell what mostly used to be called spatlese trockens. These are much more affordable and still have good fruit. At traditional 3 tier retail, the best ones are $35-$40. Through retailers that buy direct they are $25-$30. Someone mentioned Wagner Stempel Vom Porphyr, I like that very much (not one of our producers) and it’s a good dry German to try that is at traditional retail.

The cheaper dry wines, in my experience, are often more mineral and acid driven. So if you are looking for a wine with more noticeable fruit, they may not be the best wines to try as they can be a tad austere. This is a generalization, of course.

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked: I’m a little surprised by the negative comments about Große Gewächse. They’re obviously made in a fundamentally different style than the Prädikat off-dry wines, but I think both styles have their appeal. Mature Große Gewächse and their non-VDP-equivalents from great vineyards and great producers (e.g. “Bluechips” like Dönnhoff Hermannshöhle, Emrich-Schönleber Halenberg, Bürklin-Wolf Kirchenstück, Keller Morstein and Abtserde, Wittmann Morstein, Koehler-Ruprecht Saumagen Auslese tr. “R”, Breuer Schlossberg, Weil Gräfenberg, Schäfer-Fröhlich Felseneck) are - for me at least - among the great dry white wines of this planet, at least with proper bottle age (meaning at least ten years in bottle). I would agree that the GG style is only semi-suitable to Mosel, Saar and Ruwer, but in the other regions - at least in my view - quite a few producers master the dry, powerful style very well these days. There are three problems and those may lead to the many negative comments - 1. the wines are fairly expensive relative to off-dry Kabinett and Spätlese Rieslings from the Mosel, Saar, Ruwer and also Nahe. 2. they haven’t found their proper place at the dinner table yet. 3. there are fewer producers and wines making Riesling Großes Gewächs in consistent and outstanding quality than there are producers making off-dry Riesling Kabinett, Spätlese and Auslese in such quality. This leads to a few disappointments.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Yeah, I have to chime in in defense of GGs.
To say that " GGs are a waste of good grapes. The more I think about it, the more I am convinced they are simply ridiculous caricatures of riesling. Like Parkerized Bordeaux, but just in the opposite direction. How much fruit can we strip out of a wine? And how much acid can we shove in it?" is itself caricature, one that shows how out of touch you likely are with the diversity that exists within the category.
Like all wine, balance and complexity comes from a harmonizing of elements, so a dry wine with no fruit and too much acid is, well, not balanced. I know that you love RS, Adrian, but to dismiss the entire category in such binary fashion is not a very nuanced position.
As to their price, it seems a perfectly normal expectation that a grower would hope to sell a bottle of their best dry wines for 15-25 Euro (Auslese or spätlese trocken historically, which were always more expensive like their auslese off-dry counterparts) from their best sites. Continuing in the US centric view, people don’t bat an eye when considering a great white burgundy 1er cru for $40-70. Why should the Germans (who have already proven the market viability of such wines at home) be any different?
I would argue that the Kabinetten and Spätlesen are UNDERpriced, more than I would argue that GGs are overpriced.
I love them all, dry and off-dry, because beautiful is as beautiful does.
To only have one or the other would be a pity…

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Robert Panzer:

Yeah, I have to chime in in defense of GGs.
To say that " GGs are a waste of good grapes. The more I think about it, the more I am convinced they are simply ridiculous caricatures of riesling. Like Parkerized Bordeaux, but just in the opposite direction. How much fruit can we strip out of a wine? And how much acid can we shove in it?" is itself caricature, one that shows how out of touch you likely are with the diversity that exists within the category.
Like all wine, balance and complexity comes from a harmonizing of elements, so a dry wine with no fruit and too much acid is, well, not balanced. I know that you love RS, Adrian, but to dismiss the entire category in such binary fashion is not a very nuanced position.
As to their price, it seems a perfectly normal expectation that a grower would hope to sell a bottle of their best dry wines for 15-25 Euro (Auslese or spätlese trocken historically, which were always more expensive like their auslese off-dry counterparts) from their best sites. Continuing in the US centric view, people don’t bat an eye when considering a great white burgundy 1er cru for $40-70. Why should the Germans (who have already proven the market viability of such wines at home) be any different?
I would argue that the Kabinetten and Spätlesen are UNDERpriced, more than I would argue that GGs are overpriced.
I love them all, dry and off-dry, because beautiful is as beautiful does.
To only have one or the other would be a pity…

There are some great GGs. But when one compares the pricing to great pradikat wines or great Austrian dry riesling it almost never seems worth the money. And there are a lot of GGs that I just don’t like at all or find ordinary but which are priced up there with the great ones because they say GG on the label.

That said I still remember a 2006 Keller Morstein GG which was fantastic. It’s also a wine that seems pretty much unavailable in the US. I don’t even know what pricing is supposed to be like.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
There are many producers making dry wines now that are not very good, possibly at the expense of pradikat wines they do better. And there are some GGs which are priced higher than they should be. But I’m in the camp that believes there are several producers in the Nahe and Rheinhessen who are making some of the greatest white wines in the world. I think most of prices are justified when put in that context. If you like the off-dry just as much are more, then I can understand not wanting to pay more for the GGs (we’ll leave aside the fact that they serve a different food-pairing purpose). But knocking them because they are priced higher than their off-dry cousins is, to me, off base, as the two are not at all interchangeable.

Personally, I enjoy spatlese and kabinett, but don’t care to drink them very often, whereas I want to drink great dry Riesling all the time. That part of the equation is personal preference, which is very important, but not grounds for objective criticism. I can’t criticize Egon Muller TBA just because I don’t particularly like sweet things. Objectively speaking, I simply can’t agree with all dry Riseling being a “waste of grapes.” The best GGs from Keller, Ratzenberger, Schafer-Frohlich, Emrich-Schonleber and a few others are objectively complex, balanced, interesting, versatile, age-worthy and transparent. Not to mention possessed of gorgeous, joyous fruit. We also happen to love them, drink them constantly, and happily buy them at prices which, in the grand scheme of my wine habit, I consider ridiculously affordable.

Shameless self serving plug here: Bürklin Wolf is not known to Americans very well as their USA distribution has been way spotty at best with massive domestic and international demand lessening their need/desire to be present in the USA. I have changed that, bringing them in for the first time with the a smattering of 2014s, with the '15 offer to follow later this summer. Everyone under the sun (that I can think of) recognizes their nobility with quotes like:
“In that discipline (dry Rieslings), this estate has essentially no rival in all of Germany.” - Joel Payne
“…this may well be the best and most consistent producer of dry Riesling in the world.” -Joel Payne
“…this estate has once-again demonstrated that it is among the half dozen or so most consistently successful in Germany at rendering well-balanced, completely dry Rieslings capable of dancing, not to mention of distinctively evoking the top Pfalz vineyards in which they grew.” David Schildknecht

ps-one other interesting factoid: Bürklin adopted their own classification system at the turn of the Millenium, not waiting for the rest of Germany to figure out how they want to classify/market themselves…they chose the Burgundian model of villages, 1er cru, and Grand cru, which they write PC for premier cru, and GC for Grand cru.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I attended the Howard Ripley tasting in London yesterday, and was very impressed. It was nice to chat with a few of the growers, who are tangibly excited by the quality of what they have produced, albeit a bit more reserved in expressing it than anyone who spends time around the Bordelais would be used to.

Among a lot of brilliant wines, Zilliken’s really stood out for their energy and purity / clarity, even at the highest prädikat levels.

I took note of Julian Haart, whose wines I don’t know that well. His Schubertslay Kabinett was magical, and is a must-purchase for me.

The Prüm cask samples were pretty reduced aromatically, but seem very solid, with GH and WS nicely texturally differentiated and the aromatics unfolding if you took the time. The 04 WS Auslese that Katharina was pouring too was showing very nicely as well, incidentally. She cautioned against getting 2015 tunnel vision, which was very sanguine given how well the samples showed.

Strong showing from Willi Schaefer too, as usual one of my favorite addresses. Hard to chose between the Himmelreich and the Domprobst, so will probably have to buy both.

Only three Dönnhoff wines were shown but all seemed pretty brilliant to me. So I don’t really relate to the skepticism that several folks have voiced about them.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
I’d never accuse you of not knowing anything about wine. And I can’t argue with what you like and don’t like, as matters of opinion are inarguable. I myself don’t like grenache, even though I acknowledge that there are some very fine wines made from that grape. I just disagree that the great GGs (I’ve already listed those producers I admire) are caricatures, as that translates to out of balance and extreme. Those I have mentioned have not been stripped of fruit, nor have their acid levels been cranked up out of control. I find them to be beautiful, balanced, harmonious, elegant wines which display decided Riesling character traits and are transparent to their justly revered vineyard sites. The comparison with Parkerized Bordeaux or CA cabs does not work, because I don’t think even those who love those wines would deny they are larger than life, to put it kindly.

As I said earlier, there are absolutely some among the GG crowd which do fit your description. My tolerance level is intrinsically higher than yours, but I am certainly aware of those fruitless acid-heavy, though often highly-lauded, GGs that are an assault on the pallet. But, evaluating as objectively as possible, not all GGs are like that. It seems, Adrian, that you yourself agree with this. Looking at what you’ve written, if you can make an exception to your rule for Keller (putting aside the question of price), then that should logically open the door for the possibility that others might please you as well.

I really don’t care whether you like dry Riesling or not. I do take issue with the assertion that all GGs are overblown “caricatures”, unworthy of respect and “ridiculous,” and the associated implication that no true lover of Riesling (those whose beloved spatlesen and auslesen are being raped and pillaged by the GG movement) should admire and enjoy them.

posts.name: Joseph Rater
posts.cooked:
I recently tasted wines presented when the Terry Theise traveling road show came to my town. Really liked the Donnhoff 2015 Felsenberg GG - good palate coverage and length, lots of dry mineral extract, plus a cool minty floral/herbaceous note. If the trocken wines in general show more straight and less nuanced IMHO than wines with some RS, this was very enjoyable. Oberhauser Leistenberg Kabinett was richer than average, but had great balancing acidity (I ordered some) After these two, the Brucke spatlese was a bit of a letdown. More complex and nuanced than the Leistenberg, but the midpalate seemed a little thin in comparison, and I wanted a bit more acidity.

I particularly liked the Selbach Oster Zeltinger Sonnenuhr * Spatlese. Elegant, nicely balanced. Another highlight was the Von Winning table. 2015 Grainhubel barrel sample showed an almost Mosel like delicacy and nuance while retaining some Pfalz richness. 2013? Ungeheuer was deeper, more powerful but still somewhat closed. Berserkers who find GG wines too austere or shrill may want to try these. Great purity of fruit, richer but no flab either. While I liked the Donnhoff Felsenberg too, that one struck me as more for rock hounds.

posts.name: Veronica Castro
posts.cooked:
From the retail front, based on what I have tasted, many of the 2015’s are great and stunning wines. Having attended a March preview tasting in which Rudi brought back a smattering of 2015 Rieslings from his Germany trip, and then again in May for the official Wiest tasting, the 2015s in general were intense and piercing. Good acid with rounded, opulent fruit.
Standouts were the Schloss Lieser wines, and anything and everything from Schafer-Frohlich. Looking back at my tasting notes, my reaction to the Lieser 2015 Niederberg Helden Spatlese was, “like a sugar bullet to the brain.” The Lieser wines are just beautiful and jubilant. You taste them and love them through and through. The Schafer-Frohlich wines are equally compelling. They just quietly grab you and shake you and command your attention. Tim Frohlich harnessed the power of the 2015 vintage so masterfully and his wines are pure, focused, precise, gorgeous. All of that. Just a pleasure to taste.

Though I would not say 2015 is “the ultimate” (and there are always exceptions, for instance, I prefer the 2014 Gunderloch Jean Baptiste Kabinett over the 15, because of its elegance and sprightly quality); they are great, and the best I’ve had in some time. There should be wines in every pradikat for one to like, and even outside of that, as shown by Peter Lauer’s Barrel X '15, which is awesome. I can say so far, I’ve liked the 2015s better than '10, '12, '02, '07, '09, '05…some vintages I’ve really loved. Sadly, I did not taste any 2001s on release, so I cannot draw such a comparison. I asked Brad Kane, a friend whose palate I trust, about the 2015 Theise tasting he attended in NYC, and he said that 2015 is excellent, just overall not the same heights as 2001 limited to what he tasted.

Tomorrow (Monday) I’ll be attending a luncheon with Thomas Haag of Schloss Lieser. I’ll ask him his thoughts on this subject and will report back. Tuesday is the Terry Theise tasting in LA. Looking forward to it.

Cheers,

posts.name: Dirk Brinkmann
posts.cooked:
Hello everyone, my first post in this forum starts with my 2 Cents of the 2015 vintage refering only to Riesling:

I have tasted quite a few Rieslings of 2015 from Breuer, Ress, Keller, Weil, Dönnhoff and others. What really makes me believe that this could be a very good vintage? It is the perfect combination of ripe, high acídity in combination with clear, ripe fruit. In contrary to 2003 and even 2005 the acidity seems much higher, making the wines more elegant and lively. The alcohol Level seems to be quite modest too, so no real “beasts”. Most of the higher Prädikate (Auslese, BA, TBA) are made without or with very few botrytis, so the wines are very pure and focused.
But I totally agree with Phil, only time will tell, if it is really a “stellar vintage”:

Phil Bernstein:

Lots of interesting stuff here. I’ve been going to these German trade shows going back to the 01 vintage. If I’ve learned one thing it’s to wait until the wines arrive stateside before issuing opinions. These wines will change quite a bit. Tasting them at this point in their evolution is nothing more than a fun guessing game.

And some of my favourite German Riesling experiences came from “none stellar” vintages like 2004, 2006 and 2010.
So we all have to wait and see, what happens.

But one thing is certainly for sure:
I have never seen a German vintage in the past 10 years which was so quickly sold out (at least in Germany)! The top wines from Keller, Zilliken et al were sold out before their usual price lists were published. So at least for the winemakers 2015 will be a stellar vintage…

Cheers,
Dirk

Thanks for your input and your post, Phil. Big fan of you and what you’ve done at MacArthurs.

Thomas Haag came to visit LA on the hottest day of the year so far, but his crisp Rieslings saved the day.
We tasted six of his 2015 Rieslings, all fantastic, with his 2015 Wehlener Sonnenuhr Kabinett showing so beautifully. Powerful fruit supported by racy acids making it kind of crispy and straightforward.

I asked Thomas what he thought about the 2015 vintage, he said (paraphrasing) it was really too early to come out with any declarative statement, but it was an excellent vintage with many facets. Good acids, but not as high as 2010, and ripe fruit, but not as opulent as 2009. Creamier than 2008 and 2006 and 2004.

At the Terry Theise tasting yesterday, I asked Johannes Selbach the same question. He said that 2015 is a great vintage and compares it to 1990. He said that in comparing 2015 to 2001, 2001 is tame.

I also asked Andreas Spreitzer about 2015, and he compared it to 2012, his favorite vintage. He said (again, paraphrasing)… I’m not going to B-S you about “best vintage ever.” It’s too soon to say. He was very happy about his line-up.

Great showing at the Thiese tasting. My “Wow” moment was tasting the 2015 Merkelbach Urz Wurz Riesling Spatlese #1 and the 2015 Donnhoff Niederhauser Hermannsshohle Spatlese was incredible/powerful. A strong showing all around.
Cheers!

Price hikes are only to be expected when people start hyping wines or vintages up as “the best ever”, “the Holy Grail” and other such infantile nonsense.
I don’t really have a problem with price hikes per se. When a wine I’m interested in becomes so expensive that I can no longer afford it or I think it is no longer worth it, I just turn elsewhere. One of the wonderful things about wine’s diversity is that one only needs to be willing to look around a bit, or sometimes to delve a little deeper, to find something interesting.
I just find it hard to believe that anyone who’s been around for a while and is still in their right state of mind can fall for such silly hyperbole as “the best ever”, “the Holy Grail”, etc.
Having said that, luckily, I don’t think the hype has significantly affected pricing in Germany this year. I bought a fair bit of 2015 there between May and July (from producers and retailers) and did not notice anything earth-shattering (no more than marginal price hikes here and there, at worst).

I have visited regularly starting with the 2011 vintage and I had a clear impression of this (2015) being the “best” one. I put best in quotation marks, because I am not particularly qualified to make such a statement and I guess that to arrive at such a conclusion we will have to wait and see. So what I mean is that at this early stage, this vintage appears to be the best balanced of the recent ones and most wines seem to be very enjoyable already now, even though one can clearly see their huge potential. Having experienced the heat of the summer last year I was a bit skeptical about claims on the high acidity of 2015 wines, but different producers said that the acidity level were in many cases very close if not identical to those of 2010, with the difference that in 2010 one could clearly perceive the high acidity, whereas in 2015 it is well integrated from the very start. This was my impression too.

Now, concerning the Leistenberg Kabinett, I must say that in past years I wasn’t sure about which one I preferred of the two Kabinetts of Doennhoff, but this year I got a clear impression of a gap between the two, with the Leistenberg being the more serious, more complex and in general higher-potential wine among the two. I liked it very much and did not experience at all what you describe about the mid-palate and finish.

In general I was very impressed with the whole range of wines. Both with the RS and the dry ones.
Their GGs in 2015 are stunning. IMHO the best ones at this stage of the last five vintages (unfortunately I haven’t tasted earlier vintages).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Just chiming in after a few days in Germany. Tasted all the Keller 15 GGs, which are frankly as good a set of young wines from any producer and vintage as I’ve ever tasted. Really phenomenal stuff. Also tried some terrific stuff from Seehof in the Rheinhessen and Schlossgut Diel in the Nahe.

I am beginning to feel pretty complacent about the quality of the vintage. Very happy to be stocking up. Keller GGs were, felicitously enough, offered in the UK today.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: Now that I am starting to taste some of the wines as they hit the U.S. I have noticed that they seem coiled up. What I mean is different than a shut down phase where the wine is simply not giving anything. Rather the wines seem great but are holding back everything they have to offer like there is a 5th gear.

Martin Zwick:

Here is a statement from the Leistenberg thread:
(…)
In general I was very impressed with the whole range of wines. Both with the RS and the dry ones.
Their GGs in 2015 are stunning. IMHO the best ones at this stage of the last five vintages (unfortunately I haven’t tasted earlier vintages).

Martin, yes my statement would belong here as well…
Let me then add a brief description of one little experiment I did at home to test whether I could see the greatness of the 2015 vintage.
As soon as I got hold of the first wines (two Kabinetts) of the celebrated 2015 vintage I decided to try them blind against a 2014, just to understand how much better 2015 was, and see if I liked it (as I wrote above, I was skeptical about the acidity). The two 2015 were a Willi Schaefer Domprobst and a Weiser-Kuenstler Ellergrub. The 2014 again a Willi Schaefer Domprobst. This was at home, just for fun, myself and my wife, so no point in making it more extended and scientific (even though in retrospect, it would have been even more instructive to have the 2014 of the Ellergrub as well). I found two of the three wines very similar in character and the third one clearly different. Given how praised 2015 has been I concluded that the two similar ones had to be the 2015s: wrong, they were the two Willi Schaefer. After unblinding them and following them over several more days I could better focus on the difference between the two Domprobst and have no doubt that 2015 is the better version of this wonderful wine. In this 2015 kabinett there was a magic mix of ripe fruit and electric acidity, whereas the 2014 version had less ripe fruit, but also high and well integrated acidity, with the same deeper tones of the Domprobst vineyard. Anyway it was not like night and day, and again, the differences to another vineyard and producer were much more evident.

Gilberto, I would only add that 2014 is a year older of course. But I’m not surprised by your blind tasting of two of my favorite growers’ wines. I really like Willi Schaefer’s 2015 Graacher Himmelreich Kabinett and Weiser-Künstler’s 2015 Wolfer Sonnenlay Kabinett as well.

In spite of all the rain this summer, 2016 can be very good vintage. We’ve had beautiful weather the last couple of weeks.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
So my local guy has all the 2015 Prums. Lots of “buy opportunities” for sure, but my poor little country lawyer wallet only extends so far. How would you rank the Kabinetts and Spats say on a 1, 2 3, etc basis? Or perhaps I just grab a smaller lot of all of them.

Thanks all!

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Sonnenuhr, just buy them all.

Leaves more Graacher Himmelreich for my wife and me.
